ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic waffle entry (FOODON_03315091); no specific kaya-filled waffle entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers waffles generically; no kaya-specific sub-code exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for kaya waffle or generic waffle food item identified in published SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one kaya waffle of approximately 100 g, as commonly sold at Singaporean kopitiams and hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Waffle (kaya) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists 'Waffle (kaya)' as a named entry (cr_id F11100031) with all core macros and secondary nutrients. Values used directly as reported. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 5.5 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Waffles, plain, prepared from recipe' (FDC ID 172286) adjusted for kaya filling contribution, documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for kaya waffle found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 76 is assigned based on the closest analogous entry: 'Waffles, Aunt Jemima' (GI 76, glucose reference) as published in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Kaya filling adds concentrated sugar which is unlikely to lower the GI; the estimate is therefore conservative. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (76 × 49.96) / 100 ≈ 38.0 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (21.75 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.68 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium itself is moderate at 112 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit kaya waffle intake because the high sugar load (21.75 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and the saturated fat from kaya and butter topping elevates cardiovascular risk; choose a smaller portion and avoid adding extra butter.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (76), high sugar (21.75 g/100 g exceeding the 20 g/100 g threshold), high carbohydrate (49.96 g/100 g), and low dietary fibre (1.68 g/100 g) produce a very high glycemic load of approximately 38 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kaya waffle is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high glycemic load (~38 per 100 g serving), sugar content of 21.75 g/100 g, and minimal fibre buffering; it should be avoided or strictly limited to occasional very small portions with close blood glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~5.5 g/100 g from waffle batter and coconut-based kaya, combined with high sugar (21.75 g/100 g) which promotes dyslipidaemia and endothelial inflammation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit kaya waffle consumption because the combination of saturated fat (~5.5 g/100 g from coconut-based kaya and butter) and high sugar (21.75 g/100 g) raises LDL cholesterol and promotes vascular inflammation; avoid adding butter topping and restrict to occasional small servings.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Waffle with Kaya (椰酱华夫饼) is a popular Singaporean snack consisting of a crisp, golden waffle made from a pancake-style batter of wheat flour, eggs, milk, butter, and sugar, filled or spread with kaya — a rich, fragrant coconut jam made from coconut milk, eggs, sugar, and pandan leaf. The waffle is cooked in a waffle iron until the exterior is lightly crisp while the interior remains soft and slightly chewy. Kaya contributes a distinctive sweet, coconut-pandan flavour and a smooth, custard-like texture. Traditionally served warm at Singaporean kopitiams (coffee shops) and hawker stalls, often accompanied by a slab of cold butter and a soft-boiled egg, the kaya waffle is a beloved breakfast and snack item across all ethnic communities in Singapore. Nutritionally, a 100 g serving provides 361 kcal, 6.21 g protein, 15.22 g fat, and 49.96 g carbohydrate with 21.75 g sugar and only 1.68 g dietary fibre. The high sugar content (21.75 g/100 g) and high glycemic index (~76) make this food unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose. The saturated fat from coconut-based kaya and butter topping raises cardiovascular concern. Sodium is relatively low at 112 mg/100 g. This food is vegetarian and generally considered halal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a kaya waffle?
What is kaya waffle made of?
Is kaya waffle high in sugar?
Is kaya waffle suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database under the exact named entry 'Waffle (kaya)' (cr_id F11100031) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry and documented. GI estimated from the closest approved analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (5.5 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entry 'Waffles, plain, prepared from recipe' (FDC ID 172286) adjusted upward for the coconut fat contribution of kaya filling; HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, water_g, and all vitamin and mineral fields beyond potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, moisture, and micronutrient data for this specific kaya waffle preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 3. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate based on 'Waffles, Aunt Jemima' from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for kaya waffle exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Halal status is assumed based on typical ingredients; individual product certification should be verified. 5. The input health_suitability entries for 'rheumatoid_arthritis' referenced trans fat of 0.1 g/100 g; this value is not reported in the HPB source and has not been carried forward as it is unsupported.
